title: Versioning differences podman v/s python-podman

comments.body: If they are going to be independent then fine. But why are they going to have different versions?  Seems simpler to keep them the same?
@jwhonce would prefer they be separate, given that the Python bindings could be changed independently of Podman and libpod (multiple releases for a single release of Podman for example)

If we do that I think they should move to totally different Repos then.  My concern is we end up with  a test matrix.  If I update podman and there is no new version of python3-podman, will python3-podman break?

My concern was version #  gaps if we start pushing content to places like pypi.  We can state that all versions are pushed even if the client content doesn't change.  Then having one version number won't confuse people.

> If we do that I think they should move to totally different Repos then.

I'm in favor of separate repos for the Go server and the Python client.  Python bugs and/or breaking changes should not trigger Go releases, and vice versa.

> My concern is we end up with a test matrix. If I update podman and there is no new version of python3-podman, will python3-podman break?

This is a varlink-API-stability issue.  If we major-bump podman on breaking varlink API changes, then updating podman across a major version without also updating to a compatible python3-podman might break your system.  That's what major bumps are for, so I'm fine with that.  Are we explicitly covering the young varlink API in our SemVer versioning?  I can't actually find docs claiming that libpod is semantically versioned at all, let alone which APIs would be covered by that versioning...

I think we need to clarify the versioning for libpod/podman as part of this. Right now, we're just doing timed releases, with no guarantees of any sort of stability (we've avoided breaking changes thus far, but until we merge back into CRI-O, I don't think I'm comfortable making that a guarantee).

> Right now, we're just doing timed releases, with no guarantees of any sort of stability...

From [the SemVer spec][1]:

> Major version zero (0.y.z) is for initial development. Anything may change at any time. The public API should not be considered stable.

So you can adopt SemVer, stick to 0.x.y as long as you like, and basically do whatever you want (although personally I like to use the minor release number to mark pre-1.0 breaking changes).  Once you're comfortable with stability, you can cut a 1.0.0 and start being more careful.

[1]: https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html#spec-item-4
we have agreement on how this works. ... versioning change/split when we cut podman-1.0.  at that time, the python code will also go to a different repo.
